stories.headline: Pakistan says 'final text' of US-Iran war deal reached; Trump disputes leaked terms as Tehran says no final decision
stories.summary: Pakistani PM Shehbaz Sharif said June 12 that mediators reached a 'final, agreed-upon text' to end the US-Iran war, with a possible signing in Geneva as soon as Sunday. Iranian state media described a 14-point draft reopening the Strait of Hormuz within 30 days, releasing about $24bn in frozen assets and waiving oil sanctions during a 60-day talks window. Trump rejected the leaked Iranian terms as bearing 'no relation' to what was agreed, and Tehran said it had reached no final decision.

perspectives.keyFraming: A deal text is reportedly reached, yet Washington and Tehran publicly contradict each other on its substance.
perspectives.summary: Al Jazeera and NBC News reported the mediator's confirmation of an agreed text even as the two governments openly disputed the terms. A US official said the deal would dismantle Iran's nuclear program and reopen Hormuz, while Iran insisted nothing was finalized and the signing venue and timing remained unconfirmed.

sources.title: Pakistan says 'final, agreed upon' text of Iran war ceasefire deal reached
Live updates: Pakistan says U.S.-Iran deal text reached; Trump hits out at Iranian reports
Proposed Iran-U.S. deal would reopen Hormuz strait and lift oil sanctions, Iran state media says

perspectives.keyFraming: Iranian and Russian state outlets stress Tehran made no compromise on red lines and blame US contradictions for the turbulence.
perspectives.summary: RT amplified Iran's foreign ministry saying no final agreement exists and that 'contradictory' US positions have repeatedly disrupted the process. Iranian media detailed the draft's roughly $24bn asset release and oil-sanctions waivers while insisting the nuclear program was untouched.

sources.title: No final agreement on deal with US – Iran's Foreign Ministry
Iran media says draft US deal sees release of $24 billion frozen assets

perspectives.keyFraming: Western outlets treat it as a tentative breakthrough contingent on a signing that has not happened.
perspectives.summary: RFE/RL reported Trump accusing Iran of leaking false details of the proposed deal, underscoring how fragile the announced settlement remains. A Geneva signing was floated for the weekend, with Vice President Vance expected to represent the US, but no text had been signed.

sources.title: Trump Accuses Iran Of Leaking False Details Of Proposed Nuclear Deal

stories.headline: Oil falls to a two-month low as Trump touts an Iran 'settlement' and Hormuz reopening; Tehran disputes terms
stories.summary: WTI fell about 3-4% to roughly $84.6 a barrel and Brent to about $87.4 — a two-month low — on June 12 after Trump said the US had reached a 'great settlement' with Iran that could be signed this weekend in Europe and reopen the Strait of Hormuz within 30 days. Iranian state media released a 14-point draft lifting oil sanctions and releasing frozen funds, but Trump disputed it and Tehran confirmed no final deal. US forces downed two Iranian drones near the strait that morning.

perspectives.keyFraming: Markets are pricing genuine de-escalation; falling crude eases the inflation shock even as the deal stays unsigned.
perspectives.summary: CNBC and Reuters reported crude sliding to its lowest since April as traders bet a US-Iran settlement would reopen Hormuz and unwind the war premium. OPEC separately cut its 2026 demand-growth forecast to about 970,000 bpd, adding to the bearish tone.

sources.title: Oil prices fall on hopes of U.S.-Iran deal despite Tehran pushback
Oil extends losses as Trump calls off planned strikes on Iran

perspectives.keyFraming: Trump's settlement claim is credited with crushing the war premium and bringing relief at the pump.
perspectives.summary: Business coverage tied the slide to the prospect of Hormuz reopening and oil sanctions lifting, with the Washington Examiner noting OPEC's second straight demand-forecast cut as the cartel caught down to a war-hit market.

sources.title: OPEC cuts oil demand forecast again as Strait of Hormuz closure continues

perspectives.keyFraming: The rally rests on an unsigned deal; Tehran's pushback and a fresh maritime clash show Hormuz is still contested.
perspectives.summary: RFE/RL stressed that Iran disputed the leaked draft and that US forces downed two Iranian drones near the strait that morning after Iran fired on a transiting vessel, leaving the chokepoint effectively closed despite the price drop.

sources.title: Trump Accuses Iran Of Leaking False Details Of Proposed Nuclear Deal

stories.headline: SpaceX surges in Nasdaq debut after record $75B IPO, briefly topping a $2.2 trillion valuation
stories.summary: SpaceX began trading on the Nasdaq under SPCX on June 12 after pricing its IPO at $135 a share and raising about $75 billion — the largest IPO on record, valuing the firm near $1.75 trillion. The stock opened around $150 and ran as high as $168.75, up roughly 25%, lifting its market cap toward $2.2 trillion. About $15 billion of the raise came from retail investors, anchoring a wave of mega-AI and tech listings that some analysts warn could mark a market top.

perspectives.keyFraming: A historic raise lands amid a frothy AI-IPO rush that may signal late-cycle euphoria.
perspectives.summary: CNBC and CBS reported SPCX popping nearly 30% intraday on its first session after the biggest IPO ever, with NPR noting the debut sits alongside OpenAI's confidential filing and Anthropic's reported preparations in a roughly $3.6 trillion combined listing pipeline.

sources.title: SpaceX IPO live updates: SPCX pops almost 30% after biggest IPO ever
SpaceX shares soar following record-breaking $75 billion IPO
It's SpaceX's first day on the stock market

perspectives.keyFraming: Trillion-dollar floats concentrate market risk, and a flurry of mega-IPOs is a classic market-top tell.
perspectives.summary: Analysts cited by CNN and CNBC warned that simultaneous record listings from SpaceX, OpenAI and Anthropic concentrate risk and historically cluster near tops, landing during post-Broadcom chip volatility and a fragile geopolitical backdrop.

sources.title: OpenAI IPO: Major AI companies will soon be put to the test

stories.headline: IAEA Board passes US/E3 resolution demanding Iran declare and allow verification of enriched-uranium stocks
stories.summary: The IAEA's 35-nation Board of Governors passed a US/UK/France/Germany resolution by 21 votes to 3 with 10 abstentions, telling Iran to provide complete information on its nuclear-material inventories and grant inspector access 'without delay.' The agency assesses Iran holds about 440.9 kg of uranium enriched to 60%, unaccounted for since strikes on its enrichment plants. The vote landed as US-Iran talks on a broader settlement advanced.

perspectives.keyFraming: The Board demands accounting for Iran's near-weapons-grade stockpile as diplomacy over the war runs in parallel.
perspectives.summary: Middle East Eye reported the resolution passing 21-3 with 10 abstentions, ordering Iran to report uranium stocks and restore inspector access. The IAEA has been unable to verify roughly 440.9 kg of 60%-enriched material since the enrichment sites were struck.

sources.title: IAEA board passes resolution demanding Iran report uranium stocks

perspectives.keyFraming: The E3 frame the resolution as upholding the non-proliferation regime and inspector access.
perspectives.summary: A UK government statement said the E3 and US sought full Iranian cooperation with the IAEA 'without delay,' casting the measure as a defence of safeguards obligations rather than an escalation.

sources.title: NPT Safeguards Agreement with Iran: Resolution to the IAEA Board of Governors, June 2026

perspectives.keyFraming: Washington presents the vote as pressure to force Iranian transparency on its nuclear material.
perspectives.summary: JNS reported the US pushing the resolution to compel Iran to account for and grant access to its enriched-uranium inventory, tying nuclear transparency to any durable settlement of the war.

sources.title: US pushes IAEA resolution on Iran nuclear access, accounting

stories.headline: Ethiopia warns Tigrayan forces are preparing an offensive, raising fears of renewed Horn of Africa war
stories.summary: Ethiopia's government said on June 11 that hardline elements of the Tigray People's Liberation Front had 'decided to launch an offensive against the federal government in the coming days,' alleging Eritrean backing. The warning, from officials including Getachew Reda, follows months of escalating tension since clashes resumed in late January and Tigray's exclusion from the June 1 national elections. Around one million remain displaced after a war that killed an estimated 600,000.

perspectives.keyFraming: Frames a regional powder keg that could pull in Eritrea, Sudan and Gulf actors — the proxy and Red Sea dimension US wires omit.
perspectives.summary: Pan-African coverage emphasised the cross-border Eritrea accusations, the collapse of the 2022 Pretoria deal and the humanitarian stakes for a financially drained Tigray facing subsidy cuts and exclusion from national politics.

sources.title: Tigrayan forces preparing offensive against federal gov, Addis Ababa warns

perspectives.keyFraming: Surfaces the intra-Tigrayan split US coverage flattens: a deposed Tigrayan leader accuses 'power-hungry' TPLF figures of courting war.
perspectives.summary: France 24/AFP reported Getachew Reda — himself a former Tigray administrator — accusing TPLF hardliners of dragging the region toward conflict, while stressing Tigray's election exclusion, the roughly one million displaced and the unresolved political status driving the standoff.

sources.title: Ethiopia claims Tigrayan forces preparing offensive against govt
